1. Introduction
---------------

The complex relationships among growing human populations, crop yield, and [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are a matter of great agricultural importance. Demand for high crop yields to accommodate the growing global human population has risen sharply over the past twenty years [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0005). Climate change is accelerating environmental degradation, introducing numerous challenges to industrial agriculture. Whereas animals can leave their habitat to find food, plants are sessile, leaving them vulnerable to [abiotic stresses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/abiotic-stress "Learn more about abiotic stresses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as drought, increased salinity, and extreme temperatures. These stress factors can greatly reduce the vigor of crop plants, impairing their growth, development, and overall productivity. The sensitivity of yield to drought depends on the species of crop and which part of the plant is harvested. Roots, leaves, fruits, and seeds can all be affected differently, ranging from minor yield changes to complete loss of yield. Owing to its systemic damage to [plant organs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-organs "Learn more about plant organs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and physiological functions, drought is typically one of the most destructive abiotic stress events, and may be responsible for greater yield loss than that of all pathogens combined [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0010). For industrial agriculture to continue to meet the production needs of society, more research into the mechanisms of drought resistance is needed.

Drought resistance describes a plant’s capacity to recognize water-deficit stimuli and activate appropriate response pathways to preserve its viability and reproductive ability. Resistance mechanisms can be categorized as modes of escape, avoidance, or tolerance of drought conditions [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0015), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0020), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0025). Drought-escape strategies generally reduce the time required to transition from vegetative to reproductive developmental stages to ensure successful completion of the [life cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/life-cycle "Learn more about life cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or facilitate entry into a dormant state until environmental stress cues are removed [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0030), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0035). Drought avoidance involves the preservation of sufficient water in tissues to allow continued function, for example by restricting transpiration, limiting the growth and development of aerial organs, and stimulating root development to increase [water uptake](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/water-uptake "Learn more about water uptake from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") under conditions of low water availability [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0030), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0040). [Drought tolerance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/drought-tolerance "Learn more about Drought tolerance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") entails the activation of stress-responsive physiological mechanisms under water limitation, including [biosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/anabolism "Learn more about biosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of [osmoprotectant](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/osmoprotectant "Learn more about osmoprotectant from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[small molecules](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/small-molecule "Learn more about small molecules from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), increasing production of [antioxidant enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/antioxidant-enzyme "Learn more about antioxidant enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), switching phytohormone regulatory pathways, and/or expressing [molecular chaperones](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/molecular-chaperones "Learn more about molecular chaperones from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") involved in cellular [homeostasis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/homeostasis "Learn more about homeostasis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0045), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0050).

The severity of drought damage varies greatly depending on the developmental stage in which plants are affected. Seedlings are especially vulnerable, not yet having developed the long roots needed to pull water from deeper in the ground, and thus even minor periods of dehydration may result in plant death. To adapt to drought stress, plants have evolved many complex mechanisms, such as the production of metabolites that protect against stress, efficient antioxidant systems, stress-responsive hormone [signaling pathways](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction "Learn more about signaling pathways from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and changes in root and shoot morphology [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0055), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0060). Understanding how plants have evolved these strategies allows [plant breeders](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-breeders "Learn more about plant breeders from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to select for these beneficial traits and develop new drought-resistant varieties. Over the past decade, many such new varieties have been developed and released using conventional breeding strategies. While this approach has resulted in some success, these methods have failed to meet the ever-increasing demand for drought-resistant crops [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0065). Advances in biotechnology offer new, efficient, and accurate [molecular breeding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/molecular-breeding "Learn more about molecular breeding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") methods, including marker-assisted selection, genome selection, [transgenic methods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transgenic-method "Learn more about transgenic methods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and gene editing technologies [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0020).

Several reviews [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0010), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0070), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0075) have summarized the various signaling pathways and [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") manipulation-based studies in model plants. This review focuses on research advances that are opening to researchers a new window into the genetic basis of drought resistance, primarily via linkage and association mapping. In addition, this review further describes advances made in both novel and conventional approaches to accelerate drought-resistance breeding.

2. Crop response to drought stress
----------------------------------

Drought stress in crops is caused by uneven distribution and/or shortfall of precipitation combined with continuous loss of water by transpiration. Drought has many negative effects on plants, including reduced germination, suppression of leaf expansion and tillering, reduced accumulation of dry matter in organs, and reduced grain yield [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0080). To withstand these damaging effects, plants have evolved numerous physiological and molecular response mechanisms ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#f0005)A), such as rapid sensing and transduction of environmental stress stimuli, production of stress-protectant metabolites, reduction of [photosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/photosystem "Learn more about photosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and transpiration, reduced fertility of male and female gametes, and decreased grain yield [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0010), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0085). These changes can be artificially divided into two categories: rapid and long-term responses [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0090). Rapid responses are triggered at the onset of drought stress to rapidly initiate the prevention of [water loss](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/water-loss "Learn more about water loss from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by temporarily slowing metabolic processes. Long-term responses target plant functions that are less essential to immediate survival, but can result in severely diminished growth. The accumulation of [abscisic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/abscisic-acid "Learn more about abscisic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ABA) is at the core of immediate plant responses to drought. ABA [signal transduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction "Learn more about signal transduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is known [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0070), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0095) to induce rapid responses to drought stress, such as by triggering stomatal closure and the expression of stress-response genes. In the early stages of drought response, stomatal closure reduces water loss and [photosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/photosystem "Learn more about photosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") until water availability is restored [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0100), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0105). [Leaf rolling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/leaf-rolling "Learn more about Leaf rolling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is also a symptom, probably owing to the decreased cell [turgor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/turgor "Learn more about turgor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of leaf [mesophyll](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mesophyll "Learn more about mesophyll from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cells. It helps to limit transpiration by reducing the total [surface area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/surface-area "Learn more about surface area from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") exposed to the arid atmosphere [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0110), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0115), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0120). Thus, leaf-rolling and [survival rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/survival-rate "Learn more about survival rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have become common physiological indexes used for quantitative trait locus (QTL) mapping and genome-wide association study (GWAS) ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#f0005)B, C). Some examples of drought-responsive genes identified by these approaches include _ZmNAC111_[[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0125), _ZmVPP1_[[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0130), _ZmTIP1_[[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0135), _ZmSRO1d_[[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0140), _TaNAC071-A_[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0145), and _IPA1_/_OsSPL14_[[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0150).

A and B illustrate phenotypic difference between progenies of recombinant plants. “NS” and “S” represent the phenotype of “no significant” and “significant” statistic difference, respectively. (C) A simple flowchart for genome-wide association study.

The ability of drought to inflict damage on crops varies with developmental stage. In a spring with little rainfall, short-term drought reduces the rates of photosynthesis and transpiration in many crops, such as [winter wheat](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/common-wheat "Learn more about winter wheat from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (_[Triticum aestivum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/triticum-aestivum "Learn more about Triticum aestivum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ L.) and maize (_[Zea](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/zea "Learn more about Zea from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _mays_ L.) [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0080), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0155). A spring drought can lead to disease and uneven growth in seedlings, leading to a reduction in final yield. For this reason, it is essential to improve drought resistance during the primary growth phases of a crop. [Stress resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/stress "Learn more about Stress resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in seedlings can help a plant survive long enough to develop a stronger [root system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/root-system "Learn more about root system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which allows it to obtain water from a greater soil area. When a plant is in its [flowering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/anthesis "Learn more about flowering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") stage, drought can reduce pollen and ovule fertility and [seed set](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/seed-set "Learn more about seed set from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Maize is a special example of how severely a drought can damage plants’ reproductive growth. As a [monoecious](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/monoecy "Learn more about monoecious from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plant, maize develops male and female [inflorescences](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/spadix "Learn more about inflorescences from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (tassels and ears, respectively) at different positions on the plant. The tassel is positioned at the top of the plant, while the ears grow from the middle of the stalk. An ear becomes fertilized when pollen is shed from the tassel and lands on the fresh silks of ears. A drought during the maize flowering season usually extends the period between pollen shedding and silk emergence, creating a temporal barrier and leading to reduced or eliminated fertilization. This time period is known as the anthesis–silking interval (ASI) and is inversely correlated with the rate of seed setting [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0160), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0165). Drought stress also reduces the efficiency of grain filling in [cereal crops](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/cereal-crop "Learn more about cereal crops from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Quantitative traits such as ASI, number of kernels per row (KR), number of kernel rows (KRN), and 100-kernel weight (WK) can be used to evaluate maize drought resistance [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0170). These component traits are often considered to be more heritable than the final grain yield, making them useful indices of drought resistance.

The root system, with its high morphological plasticity, is the primary organ responsible for sensing and responding to drought stress. Mild drought generally restricts growth of lateral roots and stimulates growth of primary roots, driving the plant to absorb water from deeper soil layers [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0175). In irrigated farmlands, the depth of the root system affects plant productivity and drought resistance. Rice genes _DRO1_ and _qSOR1_ help the crop display higher resistance by developing deeper root systems, from [topsoil](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/topsoil "Learn more about topsoil from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") down to deeper soil layers [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0180), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0185). In contrast, a shallow root system, controlled by _EXOCYST70A3_, has advantages in obtaining water from the soil surface in regions with sparse rainfall [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0190). Root hairs also facilitate water and nutrient absorption. [Fine roots](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fine-root "Learn more about Fine roots from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") develop from single epidermal cells in the maturation zone of [root tips](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/root-tips "Learn more about root tips from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and increase the absorptive surface of a root system. _OsWOX11_ and _ZmTIP1_ can improve drought resistance by modulating root hair development [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0135), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0195). Moderate leaf rolling [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0110), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0115), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214514123000697#b0120) is a favorable trait for selection in breeding efforts aimed at improving drought resistance, along with h

enriched_points.expanded_content: Incorporating genes like _AREB_ and _DREB_ enhances drought tolerance, activating physiological pathways that regulate stomatal closure and osmotic balance, improving crop resilience under water-limited conditions.
Genetic modifications aim to develop deeper, more extensive root systems, enhancing water and nutrient absorption, crucial for sustaining plant growth during drought periods.
Transgenic crops exhibit improved water retention through the expression of proteins like galactinol synthase, which helps accumulate osmoprotectants, reducing dehydration effects.
Enhancing stomatal function via CRISPR technology optimizes water use efficiency, enabling plants to maintain photosynthesis while minimizing water loss during drought stress.
CRISPR enables precise editing of genes associated with drought response, facilitating the development of crops with targeted traits for enhanced drought resistance and productivity.
